name: Nasty Gal
summary: D2C vintage/fashion e-commerce brand that grew rapidly then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2016 amid inventory and management problems; assets were acquired by Boohoo Group in 2017.
lesson: Rapid top-line growth without inventory and operational discipline can mask an unsustainable cost structure.
source: Business of Fashion, TechCrunch coverage of Nasty Gal bankruptcy 2016-2017

name: Bebe Stores
summary: Women's fashion retailer closed all ~170 physical stores in 2017, unable to adapt its mall-based model to e-commerce and shifting consumer habits, continuing only as a licensed online brand.
lesson: Mall-anchored specialty retailers that delay e-commerce investment lose to digitally-native competitors.
source: Reuters, CNBC coverage of Bebe Stores store closures 2017

name: Charlotte Russe
summary: Fast-fashion women's retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2019 and liquidated all stores after failing to find a buyer, weighed down by debt from its 2009 LBO and declining mall traffic.
lesson: Heavy LBO debt loads leave little room for retailers to fund the digital transformation needed to survive.
source: Reuters coverage of Charlotte Russe Chapter 11 filing, 2019

name: Forever 21
summary: Fast-fashion giant filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2019 after aggressive global overexpansion left it with too many oversized stores as mall traffic and fast-fashion competition (Zara, H&M, Shein) intensified.
lesson: Overexpansion into large-format mall leases becomes a fatal liability once shopping habits and competitors shift online.
source: Wall Street Journal, Reuters coverage of Forever 21 Chapter 11, 2019

name: Le Tote
summary: Clothing rental subscription startup acquired the bankrupt Lord & Taylor in 2019, then itself filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2020 and shut down Lord & Taylor entirely, unable to sustain either business through the pandemic.
lesson: Acquiring a struggling legacy retailer to gain scale can drag down an otherwise-promising subscription model rather than strengthen it.
source: Retail Dive, CNBC coverage of Le Tote bankruptcy 2020

name: Trunk Club
summary: Personal-styling subscription service acquired by Nordstrom for $350M in 2014; Nordstrom took a $197M write-down, closed its clubhouse stores in 2020, and fully shut the service down in May 2022.
lesson: High-touch personal styling services carry stylist labor costs that rarely scale profitably even under a well-capitalized parent.
source: Business of Fashion, Retail Dive coverage of Trunk Club shutdown 2022

name: Birchbox
summary: Pioneer of the beauty subscription box model, Birchbox was sold for parts to investment firm Viking Global Investors in 2021 after years of down-rounds and failed sale attempts, well below its peak valuation.
lesson: A first-mover subscription concept can be commoditized quickly once retailers and rivals copy the model at lower cost.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Birchbox sale to Viking Global, 2021

name: Julep Beauty
summary: Seattle beauty subscription brand was acquired by Glansaol in 2016; after Glansaol's Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2018, new owner AS Beauty abruptly discontinued Julep's Maven subscription box in February 2019.
lesson: Being folded into an overleveraged multi-brand roll-up can doom an otherwise viable brand when the parent collapses.
source: GeekWire, BeautyMatter coverage of Julep Maven shutdown 2019

name: Frank And Oak
summary: Montreal-based menswear subscription and e-commerce brand filed for bankruptcy protection in 2018 after overexpanding into retail stores, and was later acquired out of restructuring by La Maison Simons.
lesson: Subscription-box brands that pivot into costly brick-and-mortar retail can outrun their cash runway.
source: Retail Dive, Fashion Network coverage of Frank And Oak bankruptcy 2018

name: Bombfell
summary: Menswear personal-styling subscription service shut down operations in 2019, unable to compete against better-funded rivals like Stitch Fix and Trunk Club in the crowded try-before-you-buy apparel space.
lesson: Under-capitalized entrants in a subscription-styling niche get squeezed out once larger players with logistics scale enter.
source: GeekWire, Racked coverage of Bombfell wind-down 2019

name: Beachmint
summary: Celebrity-endorsed fashion subscription commerce startup (JewelMint, ShoeMint, StyleMint) restructured and sold off its brands piecemeal after burning through funding without reaching sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Celebrity-branded subscription lines can drive initial buzz but rarely translate into durable repeat-purchase economics.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Beachmint restructuring 2013

name: JackThreads
summary: Men's flash-sale fashion e-commerce site was acquired by Thrillist Media Group in 2014 but was shut down/restructured in 2016 as flash-sale demand cratered and the combined company pivoted to media.
lesson: The flash-sale e-commerce category as a whole lost its differentiation once full-price sites added regular discounting.
source: Digiday, Business Insider coverage of JackThreads wind-down

name: Karmaloop
summary: Streetwear e-commerce pioneer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2015 after founder Greg Selkoe's side ventures drained cash and vendor payments fell months behind; sold to Shiekh Shoes in 2016.
lesson: Diverting core-business cash into unrelated side ventures starves the flagship company of the capital it needs to survive.
source: Boston Globe, Boston Magazine coverage of Karmaloop bankruptcy 2015

name: American Apparel
summary: Vertically-integrated 'Made in USA' fashion retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy twice (2015 and 2016) amid founder scandal, debt, and sales declines, and fully liquidated its US operations in 2017.
lesson: Founder misconduct scandals compound financial distress and can accelerate an already-troubled retailer into liquidation.
source: Wall Street Journal, Reuters coverage of American Apparel bankruptcies 2015-2017

name: Gilt Groupe
summary: Flash-sale luxury fashion site was sold to Hudson's Bay Company for just $250M in January 2016 (roughly 76% below peak valuation) as the flash-sale model lost momentum, and was resold/wound down by 2018.
lesson: A first-mover advantage in a trendy e-commerce format erodes fast once the underlying model (flash sales) stops generating repeat demand.
source: CNN Money, CBC coverage of Gilt Groupe sale to Hudson's Bay 2016

name: ModCloth
summary: Indie/vintage-inspired women's fashion e-commerce brand was acquired by Walmart in 2017, then sold at a steep loss to Go Global Retail in 2019 after failing to gain traction within Walmart's e-commerce portfolio.
lesson: A niche brand's distinct identity can get lost and its unit economics worsen when absorbed into a mass-market parent's operations.
source: TechCrunch, Retail Dive coverage of ModCloth sale to Go Global Retail 2019

name: Farfetch
funding: IPO 2018 (NYSE: FTCH), ~$2.8B in debt obligations by 2023
summary: Global luxury fashion marketplace came within days of bankruptcy in December 2023 after its stock collapsed over 95%; Coupang acquired its assets in a $500M rescue deal completed January 2024, wiping out public shareholders.
lesson: Aggressive debt-funded acquisitions (Farfetch bought New Guards Group, Browns, etc.) can turn a market leader into a forced fire sale when growth slows.
source: Business of Fashion, Puck, Forbes coverage of Farfetch-Coupang rescue deal 2023-2024

name: Matches Fashion
funding: Acquired by Frasers Group for £52M in Dec 2023
summary: UK luxury fashion e-commerce retailer entered administration in March 2024, just three months after Frasers Group bought it, cutting over half its staff and fully ceasing operations by July 2024 after years of mounting losses.
lesson: A distressed acquisition target needs a credible turnaround plan and committed follow-on funding, not just a lower price, to survive.
source: Business of Fashion, Fashionista coverage of Matches Fashion administration 2024

name: Tradesy
summary: US luxury fashion resale marketplace was acquired by rival Vestiaire Collective in March 2022 in an undisclosed-terms deal after years of losses and failed independent fundraising attempts, ending its run as a standalone company.
lesson: In a capital-intensive resale-marketplace category, consolidation into a better-funded competitor is often the only exit for the also-ran.
source: PR Newswire, dot.LA coverage of Vestiaire Collective acquiring Tradesy 2022

name: Bonobos
summary: Menswear D2C pioneer was acquired by Walmart for $310M in 2017 but never fit Walmart's e-commerce strategy; Walmart sold it to WHP Global and Express in 2023 for just $75M, roughly a 75% loss.
lesson: A D2C brand's culture and customer base can be a poor strategic fit inside a mass-market acquirer, destroying most of the deal's value.
source: CNBC, CNN Business, Fortune coverage of Walmart-Bonobos sale 2023

name: Beautycounter
funding: Sold majority stake to Carlyle Group for ~$1B in 2021
summary: Clean-beauty direct-sales brand terminated its entire consultant network in April 2024 after Carlyle Group, which bought a majority stake in 2021, decided to exit amid MLM-channel headwinds; founder Gregg Renfrew relaunched it as 'Counter' in 2025.
lesson: Direct-selling/MLM distribution channels can collapse quickly once recruitment economics turn negative, even for a well-known clean-beauty brand.
source: Business of Fashion, GeekWire, Direct Selling News coverage of Beautycounter shutdown 2024

name: Morphe (Forma Brands)
funding: Backed by Sagon Phior, funding undisclosed
summary: Influencer-driven cosmetics brand's parent Forma Brands filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2023 after closing all US stores; sales had fallen from $500M (2020) to $295M (2021), and creditors took over via a $690M debt-for-equity deal.
lesson: Over-reliance on a small set of influencer partnerships (James Charles, Jeffree Star) for brand equity creates fragile demand when influencer reputations sour.
source: NBC News, Retail Dive, Cosmetics Business coverage of Forma Brands Chapter 11, 2023

name: Outdoor Voices
summary: Athleisure D2C brand's founder Ty Haney was ousted as CEO in 2020 amid mounting losses (~$2M/month); the company later closed its stores and was acquired by Consortium Brand Partners in 2024 to avoid bankruptcy.
lesson: Public founder-board conflict combined with unchecked cash burn can force a distressed sale even for a beloved consumer brand.
source: Fortune, Inc., Retail Dive coverage of Outdoor Voices founder ouster and 2024 sale

name: Allbirds
summary: Sustainable footwear/apparel D2C brand's stock collapsed over 95% from IPO highs by 2025, with the company disclosing 'substantial doubt' about its ability to continue as a going concern before attempting a speculative pivot to AI infrastructure in 2026.
lesson: A single hero product and premium pricing rarely sustain growth once competitors copy the design and category hype fades.
source: Seeking Alpha, Forbes, Investing.com coverage of Allbirds 2025-2026 financial distress

name: CaaStle (Gwynnie Bee)
summary: Clothing-rental-as-a-service platform (originally size-inclusive rental brand Gwynnie Bee) shut down all US operations in 2025 after founder Christine Hunsicker was accused of a $530M fraud misrepresenting the company's financials to investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can instantly vaporize a company regardless of its underlying product traction or years of investor goodwill.
source: Business of Fashion, Wikipedia coverage of CaaStle/Christine Hunsicker fraud case 2025-2026

name: Glamsquad
summary: On-demand beauty services app (hair, makeup, manicures at home) shut down in 2020 after the COVID-19 pandemic eliminated demand for in-home beauty appointments and the gig-labor unit economics never reached profitability.
lesson: On-demand service marketplaces with high labor costs per transaction are especially vulnerable to demand shocks that hollow out volume.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Glamsquad shutdown 2020

name: Fab.com
summary: Design-focused flash-sale commerce site (fashion, home goods) burned through over $300M chasing hypergrowth, pivoted its business model multiple times, and was sold for parts (to PCH International) in 2015 after a failed strategy reset.
lesson: Chasing growth-at-all-costs on a flash-sale model without proven repeat-purchase economics leads to a valuation collapse when funding tightens.
source: TechCrunch, Fast Company coverage of Fab.com collapse 2013-2015

name: Toms Shoes
funding: Sold 50% stake to Bain Capital 2014; taken over by lenders 2019
summary: One-for-one D2C footwear pioneer took on debt from a 2014 Bain Capital investment; as sales declined and the buy-one-give-one model lost novelty, creditors (led by Rothschild & Co-restructured lenders) took control of the company in 2019.
lesson: A cause-marketing hook can drive initial differentiation but isn't a durable moat once competitors adopt similar giving models.
source: Bloomberg, Forbes coverage of Toms Shoes creditor takeover 2019

name: Boo.com
summary: Early online luxury fashion retailer spent $135M in 18 months on a simultaneous multi-country launch and an over-engineered website that loaded slowly for most users; it collapsed into receivership in May 2000, a symbol of dot-com-era excess.
lesson: Launching simultaneously across many markets before validating product-market fit and basic site performance multiplies the cost of failure.
source: Wikipedia, Museum of Failure, Smart Insights case study on Boo.com

name: Rent the Runway
summary: Designer apparel rental pioneer survived a rocky post-IPO stock decline and pandemic-driven demand collapse to reach adjusted profitability in 2023-2024, remaining the category leader in fashion rental-as-a-service.
lesson: A capital-intensive rental logistics model can become durable once a company reaches enough scale to spread fixed reverse-logistics costs.
source: Rent the Runway investor filings and earnings coverage, 2023-2024

name: The RealReal
summary: Authenticated luxury consignment/resale marketplace endured years of losses and a steep post-IPO stock decline before reaching its first quarter of positive adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow in 2024, validating the authentication-driven resale model.
lesson: Investing heavily in trust and authentication infrastructure can be the durable moat that eventually turns a loss-making resale marketplace profitable.
source: The RealReal investor earnings releases, 2023-2024

name: Poshmark
funding: IPO 2021 (NASDAQ: POSH), acquired by Naver Corp for $1.2B in 2023
summary: Social fashion resale marketplace built a large, engaged community-driven seller base and was acquired by South Korean internet giant Naver Corporation for $1.2 billion in 2023, delivering a strong outcome for shareholders.
lesson: Community and social-discovery features can create durable network effects that make a resale marketplace an attractive strategic acquisition.
source: Naver/Poshmark acquisition press coverage, 2022-2023

name: ThredUp
summary: Online secondhand clothing marketplace remains publicly traded and operating as of 2025-2026, having built a large managed-marketplace and 'Resale-as-a-Service' business for retail partners despite early unprofitability.
lesson: Diversifying into a B2B resale-infrastructure offering for retailers can extend a consumer resale marketplace's path to durability.
source: ThredUp SEC filings and quarterly earnings releases, 2025

name: Vestiaire Collective
summary: French luxury fashion resale marketplace consolidated the industry by acquiring US rival Tradesy in 2022 and remains a leading independent, well-funded platform in the global secondhand luxury market.
lesson: Consolidating competitors through acquisition can be a capital-efficient way to build scale in a fragmented resale market.
source: PR Newswire, Glossy coverage of Vestiaire Collective-Tradesy acquisition 2022

name: Nuuly (Urban Outfitters)
funding: Internally funded subsidiary of Urban Outfitters Inc.
summary: Clothing rental subscription service launched by Urban Outfitters grew into a meaningfully profitable, fast-growing segment of the parent company by the mid-2020s, succeeding where many standalone rental startups failed.
lesson: Backing a capital-intensive rental model with a profitable retail parent's balance sheet and sourcing relationships can outlast independently-funded competitors.
source: Urban Outfitters Inc. quarterly earnings reports, 2023-2025

name: Warby Parker
summary: D2C eyewear brand that pioneered home try-on and virtual try-on technology went public via direct listing in 2021 and has continued to expand its retail footprint and reach profitability in subsequent years.
lesson: Combining a low-cost D2C supply chain with tech-enabled try-on tools can sustainably disrupt a high-margin legacy industry.
source: Warby Parker investor relations and IPO coverage, 2021-2024

name: Glossier
summary: Beauty brand born from the Into The Gloss blog built a cult D2C following; despite 2022 layoffs and a store-strategy reset, it remains an operating, independently-run beauty company with continued product expansion.
lesson: A brand built on genuine community and content can survive strategic missteps that would sink a purely paid-acquisition D2C brand.
source: Business of Fashion, Glossy coverage of Glossier restructuring 2022-2023

name: Deciem / The Ordinary
funding: Fully acquired by Estée Lauder Companies (2021, from initial 2017 stake)
summary: Parent company of skincare brand The Ordinary built a reputation for radically transparent, low-cost 'clinical formulations,' leading Estée Lauder to acquire full ownership in 2021 after an earlier minority stake.
lesson: Transparent, ingredient-led pricing can build outsized brand trust that strategic acquirers pay a premium for.
source: Estée Lauder Companies press releases on Deciem acquisition, 2021

name: Function of Beauty
summary: Customized haircare brand that lets customers algorithmically personalize formulas took a $150M minority investment from L Catterton in December 2020 while remaining majority employee-owned, funding continued global expansion.
lesson: Mass customization backed by a simple online quiz can create a defensible personalization moat in an otherwise commoditized category.
source: PR Newswire, Business of Fashion coverage of L Catterton-Function of Beauty investment 2020

name: Ipsy
funding: $100M+ raised; merged with BoxyCharm to form BFA in 2020
summary: Beauty subscription pioneer founded by Michelle Phan merged with rival BoxyCharm in 2020 to form Beauty For All Industries (BFA), consolidating the category leader position in beauty sampling subscriptions.
lesson: Merging with a direct competitor to gain scale and shared logistics can turn two subscale subscription businesses into one durable leader.
source: TechCrunch, Beauty Independent coverage of Ipsy-BoxyCharm merger 2020

name: Fabletics
funding: Co-founded with TechStyle Fashion Group; privately thriving, $500M+ annual revenue
summary: Athleisure subscription brand co-founded by Kate Hudson built a VIP membership model that reportedly surpasses $500M+ in annual revenue and continues to expand into international markets and retail stores.
lesson: A membership model with tangible member perks (rather than pure discount subscriptions) can drive strong retention in apparel.
source: TechStyle Fashion Group company reporting on Fabletics growth, 2023-2024

name: Skims
summary: Shapewear and apparel brand co-founded by Kim Kardashian reached a $4 billion valuation in 2023 on the strength of viral marketing, inclusive sizing, and rapid category expansion into loungewear and swimwear.
lesson: Celebrity founders who deeply embed themselves in product development (not just endorsement) can build lasting brand equity rather than a fad.
source: Bloomberg, WWD coverage of Skims funding rounds and valuation 2022-2023

name: Savage X Fenty
summary: Inclusive lingerie and apparel brand founded by Rihanna reached unicorn status and continues to grow through its membership model and diverse size/shade ranges, differentiating from legacy lingerie incumbents like Victoria's Secret.
lesson: Radical size and shade inclusivity can open an underserved market segment that legacy incumbents were slow to address.
source: TechCrunch, Forbes coverage of Savage X Fenty funding rounds 2021-2022

name: StyleSeat
summary: Booking and business-management marketplace for hair and beauty professionals grew into a profitable platform serving hundreds of thousands of independent stylists and salons across the US, continuing to operate as an independent company.
lesson: A two-sided marketplace serving an underserved back-office need (scheduling, payments) for service professionals can be durable even without consumer hype.
source: StyleSeat company announcements and funding coverage, 2021-2022

name: Reformation
funding: $25M+ raised, majority stake sold to Permira 2019
summary: Sustainability-focused women's fashion brand grew from a single LA store into a national retailer, with private equity firm Permira acquiring a majority stake in 2019 to fund continued profitable expansion.
lesson: Building sustainability credibly into supply chain and brand identity (not just marketing) can command premium pricing and durable loyalty.
source: Business of Fashion coverage of Permira-Reformation investment 2019

name: Rothy's
summary: Footwear brand known for shoes knit from recycled plastic bottles reached unicorn valuation and profitability, expanding from flats into a broader lifestyle product line and physical retail stores despite 2020 layoffs.
lesson: A distinctive, sustainably-sourced material story can be a durable product differentiator rather than just a marketing gimmick.
source: Forbes, TechCrunch coverage of Rothy's growth and funding 2019-2021

name: Bombas
funding: Bootstrapped/self-funded to profitability, $1B+ in lifetime sales
summary: Sock and apparel brand built on a one-for-one donation model reached over $1 billion in cumulative sales and sustained profitability without relying on venture-scale outside funding, becoming a case study in capital-efficient D2C growth.
lesson: A simple give-back model tied to a genuinely differentiated product (blister-tab socks) can drive organic growth without heavy paid-acquisition spend.
source: Inc., Shark Tank follow-up coverage of Bombas growth 2021-2023

name: Chubbies
summary: Novelty men's shorts and apparel brand built a strong social-media-driven brand identity and was acquired by brand management firm Marquee Brands in 2021, providing a successful exit for its founders and investors.
lesson: A strongly differentiated brand voice and community (even in a mundane product category like shorts) can be an attractive acquisition target.
source: Marquee Brands press release on Chubbies acquisition, 2021

name: Tecovas
summary: D2C western boot and apparel brand grew from an online-only cowboy boot retailer into a nationwide brand with dozens of physical stores, raising growth capital at a valuation exceeding $700 million by 2022.
lesson: Cutting out traditional retail markups on a category with high perceived craftsmanship value (handmade boots) can fund rapid, profitable expansion.
source: TechCrunch, Forbes coverage of Tecovas funding rounds 2021-2022

name: Cuyana
summary: 'Fewer, better things' D2C accessories and apparel brand built a loyal following around minimalist, high-quality basics, continuing to expand its store footprint and product lines as an independent, growing company.
lesson: A clear anti-fast-fashion positioning ('fewer, better things') can differentiate a brand in an oversaturated apparel D2C market.
source: Business of Fashion, Glossy coverage of Cuyana growth and retail expansion

name: Untuckit
summary: Menswear brand built entirely around solving one product problem (shirts designed to be worn untucked) grew from an online-only startup into a national retailer with 100+ stores and continued profitability.
lesson: Solving one narrow, well-understood customer pain point precisely can be enough to build a durable national retail brand.
source: Forbes, Retail Dive coverage of Untuckit store expansion 2019-2022

name: Naadam
summary: D2C cashmere apparel brand sourcing directly from Mongolian herders built a profitable niche business by cutting out traditional cashmere supply-chain intermediaries and passing savings to consumers.
lesson: Vertically integrating a traditionally opaque, multi-layered supply chain (cashmere sourcing) can create both cost advantage and a compelling brand story.
source: Forbes, Business Insider coverage of Naadam supply-chain model

name: Curology
summary: Custom prescription skincare telehealth/D2C brand built a subscription business around dermatologist-formulated personalized treatments, growing into a profitable, still-independent company serving millions of customers.
lesson: Combining telehealth-style personalization with a subscription model can create genuine product differentiation beyond marketing claims.
source: TechCrunch, Forbes coverage of Curology funding and growth 2020-2021

name: Prose
summary: Personalized haircare brand using a detailed consumer quiz and algorithmic formulation grew into a leading player in the custom beauty category, remaining an independently operating, profitable business.
lesson: Personalization backed by real formulation science (not just marketing quizzes) can build durable customer loyalty in haircare.
source: TechCrunch, WWD coverage of Prose funding rounds and growth

name: Milk Makeup
funding: Majority stake sold to Waldencast/L Catterton-backed group in 2021
summary: Cult cosmetics brand born out of Milk Studios grew into a top-selling brand at Sephora and sold a majority stake to a Waldencast-affiliated investment group in 2021, valuing continued independent growth under new backers.
lesson: A distinct, editorial brand aesthetic incubated within an adjacent creative business (a photo studio) can translate into strong retail performance.
source: WWD, Business of Fashion coverage of Milk Makeup investment 2021

name: Too Faced Cosmetics
funding: Acquired by Estée Lauder Companies for $1.45B in 2016
summary: Cosmetics brand known for playful branding and viral product launches was acquired by Estée Lauder Companies for $1.45 billion in 2016, one of the largest indie beauty acquisitions of the decade.
lesson: Building a strong social-media-native brand voice ahead of traditional beauty incumbents can command a premium acquisition multiple.
source: Estée Lauder Companies press release on Too Faced acquisition, 2016

name: It Cosmetics
summary: QVC-driven cosmetics brand founded by Jamie Kern Lima was acquired by L'Oréal for $1.2 billion in 2016 after building a loyal customer base through direct-response TV and skincare-infused makeup positioning.
lesson: Unconventional distribution channels (home shopping/QVC) can build a large, loyal customer base that strategic acquirers value highly.
source: L'Oréal press release, WWD coverage of It Cosmetics acquisition 2016

name: e.l.f. Beauty
summary: Value-priced cosmetics brand went public in 2016 and became one of the best-performing beauty stocks of the 2020s, driven by aggressive social-media marketing and consistently strong double-digit revenue growth.
lesson: Consistently pairing low price points with viral, culturally-relevant marketing can sustain years of outsized growth in mass beauty.
source: e.l.f. Beauty investor relations, quarterly earnings coverage 2021-2025

name: Kylie Cosmetics
funding: Coty acquired 51% majority stake for $600M in 2019-2020
summary: Celebrity-founded cosmetics brand built on Kylie Jenner's social media following sold a 51% majority stake to Coty Inc. in a deal valuing the company at $1.2 billion, though later performance required Coty to write down some value.
lesson: A founder's existing social-media audience can bootstrap a beauty brand to scale far faster than traditional launches, though sustaining growth after the initial hype requires real product quality.
source: Coty Inc. press release and WWD coverage of Kylie Cosmetics stake acquisition 2019-2020

name: The Honest Company
summary: Jessica Alba's clean beauty, baby, and household products company went public in 2021 and, after early post-IPO stock declines, achieved its first full year of GAAP net income profitability in 2023, remaining publicly traded.
lesson: Clean-label positioning can sustain a public consumer brand through a rocky post-IPO period if underlying unit economics eventually improve.
source: The Honest Company investor earnings releases, 2023-2024

name: ThirdLove
summary: D2C bra and intimates brand built a fit-technology quiz and half-cup sizing innovation to challenge Victoria's Secret, weathering 2022 layoffs and a smaller cost structure while remaining an independently operating company.
lesson: A genuine product innovation (half-cup sizing) addressing a widespread unmet need can sustain a challenger brand even through a broader D2C funding downturn.
source: TechCrunch, Retail Dive coverage of ThirdLove funding and 2022 restructuring
